techniques.evidence_quote: Log4Shell payloads were used to establish connections to the server 45.147.230.219 (0x2d93e6db) at port 82
The history command also showed that the attackers' commands were successfully executed. They managed to forward the content of the stdout/stderr to the destination server 45.147.230.219
ONUS granted the AmazonS3FullAccess permission to the access key which allowed attackers to compromise and easily delete all of the S3 buckets
This backdoor was named kworker for the purpose of disguising as the Linux operating system's kworker service

The reason why they read cyclos.properties is that this file contains AWS credentials... Also on these servers, ONUS had a script to periodically back up the database to S3 which contained the database hostname and username/password
the attackers could access the ONUS database to get user information... 2 million ONUS users' information including EKYC data, personal information, and password hash was leaked
When ONUS confirmed that the user data in the AWS S3 had been deleted, we immediately planned and executed incident responses
the attackers sent a ransom request of $5M USD to ONUS via Telegram. ONUS rejected the request

attack_chain.description: Attackers exploited the Log4Shell (CVE-2021-44228) vulnerability in ONUS's Cyclos payment server between Dec 11–13, triggering outbound JNDI/LDAP connections to attacker-controlled server 45.147.230.219:82.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: Log4Shell payloads were used to establish connections to the server 45.147.230.219 (0x2d93e6db) at port 82

attack_chain.description: Attackers achieved remote code execution on the Cyclos server and ran arbitrary OS commands, with stdout/stderr forwarded back to their C2 server.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The history command also showed that the attackers' commands were successfully executed. They managed to forward the content of the stdout/stderr to the destination server 45.147.230.219

attack_chain.description: Attackers read the cyclos.properties configuration file, which contained AWS access keys (with AmazonS3FullAccess) as well as database hostname, username, and password.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The reason why they read cyclos.properties is that this file contains AWS credentials... Also on these servers, ONUS had a script to periodically back up the database to S3 which contained the database hostname and username/password

attack_chain.description: Attackers downloaded and deployed a custom Golang backdoor named 'kworker' onto the compromised Cyclos server.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the attackers downloaded and ran a backdoor on the server. This backdoor was named kworker for the purpose of disguising as the Linux operating system's kworker service

attack_chain.description: The backdoor was named 'kworker' to masquerade as a legitimate Linux kernel worker process, evading casual inspection.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: This backdoor was named kworker for the purpose of disguising as the Linux operating system's kworker service

attack_chain.description: The kworker backdoor established a persistent SSH tunnel to C2 server 45.147.230.219:81, with a SOCKS5 proxy as a fallback channel, enabling ongoing interactive access.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: It was used as a tunnel connecting the C&C server and the compromised server via SSH protocol (a wise way to avoid detection!)... SOCKS connection used as a backup when SSH is down

attack_chain.description: Using the harvested AWS access keys (with AmazonS3FullAccess), attackers authenticated to AWS and gained full control over ONUS's S3 buckets.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: ONUS granted the AmazonS3FullAccess permission to the access key which allowed attackers to compromise and easily delete all of the S3 buckets

attack_chain.description: Attackers accessed ONUS's S3 buckets and database (using credentials from the backup script) to exfiltrate ~2 million users' EKYC data, personal information, and password hashes.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the attackers could access the ONUS database to get user information... 2 million ONUS users' information including EKYC data, personal information, and password hash was leaked

attack_chain.description: Attackers deleted user data stored in AWS S3 buckets, likely to increase leverage for their ransom demand.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: When ONUS confirmed that the user data in the AWS S3 had been deleted, we immediately planned and executed incident responses

attack_chain.description: Attackers sent a $5M USD ransom demand to ONUS via Telegram, threatening to publish the stolen data. ONUS rejected the demand, after which attackers posted data on a hacking forum.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the attackers sent a ransom request of $5M USD to ONUS via Telegram. ONUS rejected the request
